original_abstract:
Risk is a fundamental and inescapable feature of human activity, particularly within the business sphere where it can never be entirely eliminated. Effective risk management, therefore, focuses on the minimization of risk and the mitigation of its negative consequences under specific conditions. Despite its importance, the classification of risk remains a complex and broad challenge in scientific literature. Various categorization frameworks exist, typically tailored to the specific objectives of the classification or the particular industry involved.

Risk categorization involves organizing identified risks into distinct classes based on shared parameters and characteristics. In this paper, the authors synthesize the theoretical foundations of risk categorization, specifically as they pertain to business activities. By reviewing established frameworks—such as clean vs. speculative and internal vs. external risks—and examining core parameters like probability, vulnerability, and asset value, this work aims to contribute to the development of risk management as a rigorous scientific discipline.
humanized_abstract:
Risk is an inherent and unavoidable element of any human action; in the field of business this element cannot be avoided or eliminated. Therefore, risk management aims at diminishing risk and, above all, at reducing possible damage in specific cases. The classification of risk, although a very significant and highly scientific issue in great depth in the literature, is an unsolved multidisciplinary problem due to its variety of aspects and possible classifications, which depend on the criteria that may vary from one field of application to another.

Risk categorization is the activity of classifying risks into certain categories based on common risk characteristics. In this paper, we summarise the theoretical approaches of risk categorization in relation to operational risk. After examining the pros and cons of the clean versus speculative and the internal versus external risk models, we analyze the common risk characteristics such as probability, exposition and value at risk. The aim of this paper is to contribute to the development of risk management into a mature science.
